next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 8

Vel counted the road south in levels.

The Spent Towns fell behind them over three days. South of the last managed farms, the trade road narrowed to dirt and the fields went from rich to merely serviceable. Spring had settled into warmth. The air smelled like cut grain and drying mud.

Twenty-eight. That number sat behind everything else.

Dren walked point. He read the track by habit: fresh ruts, local bootprints, last night's soft spots. Twenty years on the southern routes showed in how little of his attention looked busy.

"There's a depot settlement about an hour ahead," he said. "Grain storage, maybe an inn. We fill water there and keep going."

"How big?" Vel asked.

"Thirty, forty. Farms around here feed into it."

Kem walked behind her with his own pack and, for once, didn't fight the straps. He kept looking at the fields as if open land were still something he had to learn in pieces.

"Is that wheat?" he asked.

"Barley," Dren said.

"Right."

"You can tell by the awns."

"I know. Vel told me."

"Hm." Dren glanced at the field. "Charm's running thin on this stretch. Means the depot folk will be touchy about strangers near the silo."
open_hooks_to_carry: The Spent Towns as functional communities the Hoarded dismiss
Dren's introduction: practical, profane, unimpressed by birth
Kem seeing working people for the first time — people who choose to spend
Dren's daughter Mira and the economic cost of her illness
Kem's House signet draws looks in the Spent Towns — a tavern keeper sees it and her face closes; his first shame at the symbol

payoffs_to_land.description: The signet's social meaning outside the Vault — in the Spent Towns, the Hoarded crest is not respected but resented

prev_tail_excerpt:
...umber and dropping against it, the way a foot drops when it expects one more stair and finds only air.

She made herself stand.

"The target is south-southeast," Sable said. Her voice held. "Approximately nine days at our pace, assuming our pace proves measurable."

No one said anything. Five faces watched her with the politeness of people who had just seen something happen for which the Vault had given them no approved vocabulary.

"We proceed south."

They walked through the gate. Sable Orm, forty-seven years old and level 298, stepped over the Vault's threshold and into the world.

Warm air moved across her face. Real birds made noise without permission. The road under her boots was packed earth with roots lifting through it, nothing polished, nothing level, everything asking for attention. Behind her rose the walls that had contained her entire life. She did not look back.

By the first hour, the revised plan was already revising itself. Kess had bled through one heel wrap. Tarrell surrendered the notebook to Chael after ten minutes of pretending he could carry it and his pack at once. Marris could not keep the field log while walking, so the first halt came early and beside a drainage ditch instead of at any Council-approved landmark. Lenn abandoned the pace he had planned in his head. Chael, carrying half the room, was the one who never complained.

Marris opened the log on her knee. Sable dictated the entry while the Wayfinder kept humming south in her bones: first halt advanced, pace reduced, injuries minor, pursuit continuing.

The road went south. She followed it.
secondary_character_beats: Dren's agreement is explicitly transactional — the payment might cover Mira's healing, and this calculation is visible in his negotiation
state_deltas_required: Kem's privilege confronting lived reality; Dren's economic risk calculation against his family's needs
The world expands beyond the Hoarded/Spent binary; Dren introduces the human cost of level economics
